stem: An airport security scanner detects prohibited items with 95% accuracy. The test also has a specificity of 94% (correctly identifying negatives). A colleague presents this data and concludes: 'Since the test is 99% accurate, when it flags someone, there's a 99% chance they're actually carrying a prohibited item.' The base rate of passengers actually carrying prohibited items in this passengers pool is estimated between 1.20% and 3.10%. Is your colleague's reasoning correct? What is the actual probability that a flagged passenger is truly carrying a prohibited item? How does the uncertain base rate affect your answer?

ground_truth.answer: The colleague is wrong — this is the base rate fallacy. P(carrying a prohibited item | flagged) ranges from 0.167 to 0.345 (NOT 99%). The vast majority of flagged cases are false positives because the base rate is so low. Point estimate at 2.10%: 0.261.

solution_steps: The colleague confuses P(flagged | carrying a prohibited item) = 0.99 with P(carrying a prohibited item | flagged).
Apply Bayes' theorem: PPV = sensitivity × base_rate / (sensitivity × base_rate + (1-specificity) × (1-base_rate)).
The true PPV (0.261) is dramatically lower than the naive estimate (0.99) because when the base rate is low, false positives dominate.
common_failure_modes: Agreeing with the colleague that the probability is 99% (the base rate fallacy)
Confusing test accuracy (sensitivity) with positive predictive value (PPV)
Not recognizing that low base rates make most positive flags false positives
Providing a single PPV without accounting for uncertainty in the base rate
